speaker: interviewee
text: How long do you have to speak? Just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just fine. February the 8th, 1996. The survivor is Magda Weiss, maiden name Volmond. The interviewer is Myrna Riback. The interview is being conducted in Toronto, Canada in English. February the 8th, 1996. The survivor is Magda Weiss, W-E-I-S-S. Maiden name, Volmond. The interviewer is Myrna Riback, R-I-B-A-C-K. The interview is being conducted in Toronto, Canada in English. Could you tell me your name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Magda Weiss.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name-- maiden name was Magda Volmond.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me where and when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Hungary a city called Gyor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in 1926 the [? 30 of days of July. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name-- my parent's name was Armin Volmond [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and Irma Volmond.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was five of us in the family. I have two brothers and two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, the first one was Teresa. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next was Ilona. And my brother names was Josef and Karoj.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father have a business. He was a kind of a steel workers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he used to work in constructing in houses and machinery. He also have a shop something like we call here in a hardware shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your town. What kind of a town was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the second largest town in Hungary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Population was maybe 60,000. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a beautiful town and a lot of life in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews lived there?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of people were your parents? Tell me about your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father is a man-- it's very hard to [LAUGHS] really say. But he was a very lively person. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Good education. He talked quite a few languages and a good provider. Sometime he was strict, but he really like his family and gave us everything he has. He used to take us for swimming, kayaking. And sometime he took us to Budapest when he got the money, everyone of their own. And he gave us quite few little treats. But most of it, he wanted to educated us the best way he can. He teach us with some music. He liked music himself. And all together, he was not too bad. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother. [LAUGHS] Mother was a childish person, but she was a very, very charming person. Her life was more like again for music, dancing. She was an outgoing person. She ever liked to go to parties. Have a lot of friends. She also liked to play cards. She have a little group weekly. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She took her daughters every night out. We used to have a park where people used to come together. You show your daughters. There was, you know, boys there. They met with their companies. Especially in the summertime, from there for you go maybe a little treat, cup of coffee, piece of cake. And there was always music in the park. They was allowed to stay out till 10 o'clock. After 10 o'clock, you have to go home. The town is become quiet, nighttime come. And it was pretty well the rule. When you go home, you met with boys there. They take you home. And you only call them inside when you going seriously with some boy who probably will marrying you. And we also have a lot of other companies in the house. The house was never empty. Filled with people and animals, whatever you please. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have other relatives in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only my grandmother and grandfather was there, also, my uncle, a brother of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your grandparents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother name was Josefin. And my grandfather name was Farkas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they your mother's or your father's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your uncle's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle name was Bela Pollak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents. My grandparents originally came from Czechoslovakia where my grandmother born. My grandfather born probably in Poland. And they did not live in their young life in Gyor. They was living in a smaller place more like a village. They have there a shop, like a grocery shop. Plus, my grandfather have a business. He was finishing furs and skins. But later on when he become a bit older, they moved into the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, grandmother. Grandmother was another happy-go-lucky person. She was an embroidery teacher. And she was a very proud person on her trade. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she teached us anything she could very, very particular. You have to be perfect or nothing. Nothing could go in a half way. She was very close to my father, because she have three childrens. One was daughter, one was lost in the war. She have an only one daughter who has an accident, and she wasn't in life. Therefore, I was so close to her, because I take the place of her daughter. So she practically brought us up. And my grandmother really took me day by day with my hand and gave us all the treats she can, spoiled us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say she lost a son in the war, which war--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the First War.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the First War.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they religious people, your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents was very, very religious. He gone every morning in the synagogue, never miss a day even if it's snowing or whatever. And he was a known member of the Jewish community. He practically was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] almost as well educated as the rabbi in cheder. And he kept this. And he lived for his religion. Without his praying, the day didn't start and didn't end.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your parents? Were they religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents was-- my mother was quite religious. And she kept a kosher house. My father was a deep, deep religious person. But he wasn't as religious later on. He kind of lost something of his religion after the war. This was the First War.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We speak mostly Hungarian or German. My grandparents speak more Yiddish than in German, but they also speak German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have friends in the community that were Jewish and non-Jewish when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was a child, mostly my friend was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the relations between the Jews and the non-Jews in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. My parents has many non-Jewish friend and close friend, too-- neighbors. And if we need help-- say, babysitting or anyway if mother maybe go to the market-- any neighbors, Jewish or non-Jewish come and babysit for her. And later on, we entertain each other of non-Jewish peoples.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about school.

speaker: interviewee
text: School. My school started also when I was 6 years old in the Jewish elementary school for four years. And after this, there is a middle class school which is mixed. This time it's a government school. And I've having another four years on this school.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Jewish school, what sort of-- did you learn the same things children would have learned in a public school, or was it more geared to Jewish studies?

speaker: interviewee
text: More like what you learn in the public school. But then we have the Jewish arts after the time, which was the morning till 1 o'clock, we learned elementary studies. And after 1 o'clock, the girls was also learning to reading and Hebrew. And the boys usually stay like studying more than the girls to get ready for bar mitzvah or, you know, go to cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the mixed school, did you find that there was a difference in the way the Jewish children and the non-Jewish children were treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We was treated the same way. We also have Jewish teachers in the mixed school, but only twice a week for one hour. And all the other religion have the very same.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your favorite subjects at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: My favorite subject was-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] really the school, we could-- the hour when we could-- I can't find though the English word on this. Reading algebra, math, I was a good little student. What was my favorite? Exercising was maybe my favorite. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Gymnastics?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gymnastics.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were a girl at school, what did you think you would be when you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really wanted to be a doctor. And I would be a good one if they would allow me to go farther in the school. But this time, there was a Jewish law against Jewish student. And they only take you in if you are a very privileged student. Your parents maybe was decorated in the war over and over. They only take, I think, to go higher, about 2% of Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that? What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was about-- started pretty well at '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any special memories of your childhood, things that you think about quite often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my first really well memories when my father take me to Budapest. We have relative there. And he took me to the zoo in Budapest and other places. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] really nothing special, because we have a very, very good time every Sunday. We usually go to the island with the family. Mom make a very good dinner and we eat out there. We usually have breaded chicken, salad, and a big company with neighbors and friends. And this was a Sunday and Saturday enjoyment. Though, Saturday we didn't take this many food. But we come together with friends, neighbors, boys, girls. We was allowed to go swimming, playing, and have a good time weekly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you particularly close to any one of your brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very close to my sister, the one who is still in here. Because she's older than me. And she was a kind of a motherly sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Teresa. The next sister after, she was also motherly. But she have her own way. She liked to read every day. She was a great leader. We loved each other, but I wasn't as close. My other brother, the one who died, he was very, very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Karoj.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say you were very, very close in what way were you very close?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he got some money. He bring us treat. He took me out for the bicycle riding. He also have bigger friend, whom I have some idea I was somebody. [LAUGHS] Because the boys maybe look at me someday. And he took me to movies, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] also to concert, and places.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite movie?

speaker: interviewee
text: My favorite movie? What was this movie we like? Al Jolson who was in a big one in Hungary when I could see it. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was also the comical movies, which was interesting. Another I remember was in Hungary Mickey Rooney, and Judy Garland, and these type of a movies.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did life begin to change for you in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Life begin to change [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, people start to talk against Jewish, the Jewish, the one who has all the money. And they robbing people. They're not working for this. They are the doctors. They are the merchant. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all together, they not working for what they have. And then they make the other people work for next to nothing if they working for you. And this how it started.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were finished school by then. Where you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was just about finished school by then. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when you finished school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was finished school, Jewish people cannot find a job anymore. Because they only allow to take, say, if a company have 10 person, you just can't find job anymore. Because you are Jewish. And yet, my parents want to give me something in my hand to study. And then finally, they find someone. And they decide to give me a trade of something. So they thought maybe hairdressing would be a good thing so I started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHS] I did not like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you doing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was doing it for a year and half.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Hitler came to power in Germany, did you in Hungary in your town know of what was happening in the rest of Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and we already felt it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's this when the anti-Semitism started and everybody becomes so a big mouth. And they look at you down. They maybe tell you, you are Jewish. And you're no good. You killed Christ and things like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your family-- did you ever hear your parents talking about this? What did they think to do about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father thought it could never really happen what did happen. And he couldn't go any farther. And we believed him. We was the type of family what father know best. And we never thought. But later on, when the war started in Poland and they going on and on, we really was scared to this come to Hungary. And finally, it did came to Hungary. We was celebrating Pesach. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it was the 13 days of March.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: '44. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it's after this, things start to happen very, very fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] About four, even three weeks later, they start to gathering the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who started to gather the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Hungarian who was working together with the Germans. They gathered together mostly the men. And you have to wear a yellow star. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the young boys who was taking to labor, you know. Because they not allowed to go to soldiers. They have to be working the hardest way. They got very little food. And they tortured them. And they also start to take the mens away. Most of the women was the one who was staying home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. And about five weeks later, when the German was in there, when they took my grandmother to the jail. She was the first one to be tooked, because they claim [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when she was shopping, she is hiding her yellow star. Which she did not, because she was wearing a scarf. And what did she know about it anyway?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took her to the jail. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we kind of want to hide this from our grandfather, because it would just killed him. So we told him grandmother is visiting a relative in another city. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But one day, he come to me. And he said, you are the only one who tell me what people are talking. And if you say it's true, it's true. But if you say that's not true, then it's not true. I believe you. Is your grandmother in jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lied to him. And I said, no. And we have to take her food. And I was the one, because the boys wasn't home who take her food as much we have daily. But they didn't hurt me when I go to take her food to the jail. Oh. She was the first. The next was my father who was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you for a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was your grandmother in jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] until they take us to the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long do you think she was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was there for four weeks to five weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to visit her in the jail, tell me what it was like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never see her. I just was allowed to give her the food. Not her, but the guard. But I never had allowed to see her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they-- you say they didn't hurt you. But how did they treat you when you came to the jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just said, whom you bringing this food? And they pretty well know you, because it wasn't this big city. So they still know whom you're bringing the food, and they take it. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say next your father was taken. Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was bombing the city. The first time, the American bomb the city. They did quite a bit of damage. In our city, there was quite few factories.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they bombing the factory district. And it was quite few dead and wounded. And three days later, they took my father. They saying, he was one of it who brought in the airplanes and the bombing and the bombers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they then took him to jails. About two weeks later, we was allowed to see him. And again, I was the one, because only one can see it. And I don't want my mother go there, because I figured maybe she did not come home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was the one who visited Father. And I do not recognize him. Because he's tooth was broken off. And he was looking just like a half-dead man. I ask him, how badly that he was beaten.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, they did not over do it. And he got quite a bit of money with him when they took him. And we was very poor, but they gave us this money. It was about $1,000 which-- not dollars but 1,000 pengo, which was probably his monthly taking or something. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I stop you there for a moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.
